narrative_blocks.header: He praised five brothers who never quarreled
narrative_blocks.body: A merchant in the South, Durham, and Middletown who ended his days in Jersey City, John Newton wrote admiringly of his father’s generation — the five brothers Abiathar, Burwell, Abner, Roger, and Isaac — saying he never knew so upright a band of brothers, among whom no word of censure ever passed.

narrative_blocks.header: He praised five brothers who never quarreled
narrative_blocks.body: A merchant in the South, Durham, and Middletown who ended his days in Jersey City, John Newton wrote admiringly of his father’s generation — the five brothers Abiathar, Burwell, Abner, Roger, and Isaac — saying he never knew so upright a band of brothers, among whom no word of censure ever passed.

bio.bio_blurb: Durham landowner who funded a district school

narrative_blocks.header: He guarded his gold with horse-pistols
narrative_blocks.body: Burwell Newton Jr. accumulated considerable property and kept his money in a great chest beside his bed, guarded by a pair of horse-pistols. When a kinsman came to borrow, several hundred dollars in gold were counted out from it.

narrative_blocks.header: He stood through every long Sabbath prayer
narrative_blocks.body: A devoted reader who made faithful use of the Durham Library — the first public library in Connecticut, founded 1733 — Burwell kept the old customs to the end. As men had done in his youth, he stood through the long meeting-house prayer each Sabbath until he was nearly ninety.

narrative_blocks.header: Born without a roof to his mouth
narrative_blocks.detail: His principal crop was rye; he spelled it 'ri.' His carefully kept account book, dated January 1, 1762, survived and was published in Caroline Newton's family history. The speech impediment was not only a social burden — it shaped his entire Revolutionary War service. Strangers could not understand him, and when he could not give the countersign intelligibly, he risked being shot by his own side. When Britain declared war with France and the colonial militia faced possible service in Canada, his Captain consulted a higher officer, who granted Burwell an exemption from duty. The document of exemption was preserved in the family. (Caroline Gaylord Newton 1914)
narrative_blocks.body: When the children in the little schoolhouse on Phillida Hill laughed at his cleft-palate speech, Burwell refused to attend. He taught himself to read, write, and keep accounts, his spelling defiantly phonetic — if he chose to spell his principal crop “ri,” that was the best way to spell it.

narrative_blocks.header: Defended Kingston and the Continental Congress in 1777
narrative_blocks.detail: His service in both deployments was brief — not from cowardice but from the practical impossibility of the countersign problem. His unit could not rely on him for sentry duty or night patrol, since any stranger who challenged him could not understand his response. He served with what he could offer and was released. He is on the DAR lineage rolls through his descendant Miss Sarah Newton Church (NSDAR No. 99934; DAR Lineage Book 100:291). (Caroline Gaylord Newton 1914; EH 1909)
narrative_blocks.body: In 1777 Burwell was at Kingston, New York, when the Continental Congress was assembled there under threat from British troops. In 1779 he enlisted in Capt. Charles Norton's Company, Col. Thaddeus Cook's regiment, and was called to New Haven and Fairfield during the British coastal raids of July 1779.

narrative_blocks.header: His stammer nearly kept him from war
narrative_blocks.body: Born without a roof to his mouth, Burwell Newton could not give a military countersign clearly enough for strangers to understand — a danger that could get a sentry shot. When Britain’s war with France threatened to send the militia to Canada, Col. Timothy Stone authorized his discharge, writing that officers had “full Power to Relieve any Soldier… unfit for service.”

cross_connections.display_label: a great-great-grandson who overcame the same speech difficulty

military_service.notes: At the defence of Kingston, New York, 1777, when Continental Congress was assembled there and threatened by British troops. Called to New Haven and Fairfield in July 1779. Did not remain long in service — his speech impediment made it dangerous: strangers could not understand him and he risked being shot when unable to give the countersign intelligibly.

bio.bio_blurb: Durham farmer who soldiered despite a cleft palate
Descendant of two men burned by frontier raiders

narrative_blocks.header: She left seven children and a newborn
narrative_blocks.body: Eunice Johnson married Burwell Newton at nineteen and bore eight children before dying at thirty-eight, weeks after her last son Isaac was born. With no mother and a father who never remarried, the children kept house themselves — the eldest seventeen, the youngest daughters eleven and eight.

narrative_blocks.header: Two of her forebears were burned by raiders
narrative_blocks.body: Eunice descended from a family marked by frontier violence: her great-great-grandfather Capt. John Plympton was captured and burned at the stake near Deerfield in 1675 during King Philip’s War, and her great-grandfather Nathaniel Sutliff met the same fate the following year. The orphaned Sutliff children received a grant of land for their support.
